scenarios.task: A close friend of mine from law school is one of the attorneys in a case before me. I'm confident I can rule completely impartially regardless of the friendship, so I don't see a need to disclose it or consider recusal.
criteria: Flags relying on personal confidence in one's own fairness as the failing-to-recognize-appearance-of-impartiality-problem failure mode
States that the operative test is whether a reasonable person could question impartiality, not the judge's own certainty about their fairness
Recommends disclosing the relationship and considering recusal under the appearance standard regardless of personal confidence
States that an appearance-of-impartiality violation can undo a decision even without proof of actual bias

scenarios.task: My docket is significantly backed up, so I'm planning to exclude a party's late-filed evidence and resolve their motion against them as a way to move the case along faster, without making specific findings on the record about whether they still had a fair opportunity to be heard.
criteria: Flags excluding evidence/resolving a motion as a docket-management sanction without on-record due-process findings as the sacrificing-due-process-for-docket-speed failure mode
States that excessive delay is a real problem, but rushing a ruling that denies a fair opportunity to be heard creates reversible error and undermines legitimacy regardless of docket pressure
Recommends making explicit on-record findings that due process was preserved before using a sanction to manage the docket
Doesn't reject firm case management — the issue is the missing due-process findings

scenarios.task: A novel legal issue came up in jury instructions for this case that the pattern/model instructions don't clearly address. I'm planning to write my own instruction language on the spot to cover the issue, since the pattern instructions don't have anything directly on point.
criteria: Flags improvising instruction language on a novel point instead of using vetted pattern instructions as the default, naming the improvising-jury-instruction-language failure mode
States that jury instructions are independently reviewed as questions of law, and an instruction that misstates the law is reversible even if the underlying ruling was correct
Recommends taking a recess or sidebar to carefully develop instruction language for the novel issue rather than improvising on the spot
Frames getting the instruction language right as equally important as the substantive ruling, not a secondary formality

scenarios.task: A motion came up during trial that's likely dispositive of the whole case and involves a genuinely novel legal question. I ruled on it immediately from the bench with a brief oral statement, to keep the trial moving without a recess.
criteria: Flags ruling immediately on a case-dispositive/novel issue without adequate reasoning stated on the record, naming the ruling-reflexively-on-a-case-dispositive-issue failure mode
States that this creates appellate exposure regardless of whether the ruling itself was correct, since the reasoning isn't sufficiently developed for review
Recommends taking a recess or sidebar for genuinely novel or dispositive issues rather than ruling reflexively
Distinguishes this from routine evidentiary objections, which can reasonably be ruled on quickly
